perturbation.original_text: 3. Restrictions/Obligations. Recipient shall: (a) only disclose the Confidential Information to those employees and contractors with a need to know; provided that Recipient binds those employees and contractors to terms at least as restrictive as those stated in this Agreement (or, with respect to professional advisors, advises them of its obligations with respect to the Confidential Information); (b) not disclose any Confidential Information to any third party, without Discloser’s prior written consent; (c) use such Confidential Information only to the extent required to accomplish the Purpose; (d) not reproduce Confidential Information in any form except as required to accomplish the Purpose; (e) not reverse engineer, decompile, or disassemble any software disclosed by the Discloser; (f) not directly or indirectly export or transmit any Confidential Information to any country to which such export or transmission is restricted by regulation or statute; and (g) promptly provide the Discloser with notice of any actual or threatened breach of the terms of this Agreement. However, Recipient may disclose Confidential Information in accordance with a judicial or other governmental order provided that Recipient shall give Discloser written notice prior to such disclosure. Subject to Recipient’s obligations of non-disclosure, Recipient shall be free to use the residuals resulting from the use or access to the Confidential Information of Discloser. The term “residuals” means information in intangible form which is retained in the unaided memory of persons who have had access to the Confidential Information, without reference to such information in tangible or fixed form. Solely with respect to non-technical Confidential Information, Recipient’s obligations under this Section 3 shall expire one (1) year from the date of disclosure of such non-technical Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. Restrictions/Obligations. Recipient shall: (a) only disclose the Confidential Information to those employees and contractors with a need to know; provided that Recipient binds those employees and contractors to terms at least as restrictive as those stated in this Agreement (or, with respect to professional advisors, advises them of its obligations with respect to the Confidential Information); (b) not disclose any Confidential Information to any third party, without Discloser’s prior written consent, unless the third party is a potential investor; (c) use such Confidential Information only to the extent required to accomplish the Purpose; (d) not reproduce Confidential Information in any form except as required to accomplish the Purpose; (e) not reverse engineer, decompile, or disassemble any software disclosed by the Discloser; (f) not directly or indirectly export or transmit any Confidential Information to any country to which such export or transmission is restricted by regulation or statute; and (g) promptly provide the Discloser with notice of any actual or threatened breach of the terms of this Agreement. However, Recipient may disclose Confidential Information in accordance with a judicial or other governmental order provided that Recipient shall give Discloser written notice prior to such disclosure. Subject to Recipient’s obligations of non-disclosure, Recipient shall be free to use the residuals resulting from the use or access to the Confidential Information of Discloser. The term “residuals” means information in intangible form which is retained in the unaided memory of persons who have had access to the Confidential Information, without reference to such information in tangible or fixed form. Solely with respect to non-technical Confidential Information, Recipient’s obligations under this Section 3 shall expire one (1) year from the date of disclosure of such non-technical Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The modified clause in Section 3(b) adds an exception allowing disclosure to potential investors without prior written consent. This directly contradicts the general restriction on disclosing Confidential Information to third parties, as initially and broadly defined in Section 1, creating uncertainty on when information should be considered confidential and protected.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1. Definition. Subject to the limitations set forth in Section 2, all information (including but not limited to ideas, techniques, works of authorship, models, inventions, know-how, processes, equipment, algorithms, software source documents and formulae related to the current, future, and proposed products and services of each of the parties and information concerning research, development, financial information, procurement requirements, purchasing, manufacturing, customer lists, business forecasts, sales and merchandising, and marketing plans), disclosed by one party (the “Discloser”) to the other party (the “Recipient”) which is labeled or marked “Confidential” or with some other similar proprietary legend, or which, in the context of the disclosure, the Recipient has reason to believe is confidential, shall be deemed to be “Confidential Information.” If Confidential Information is disclosed in oral form, the Discloser shall identify it as confidential at the time of disclosure and thereafter summarize it in writing and transmit such summary to the Recipient within thirty (30) days of the oral disclosure.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 3(b) adds an exception, allowing disclosure of Confidential Information to potential investors without Discloser's prior written consent. This directly contradicts the initial definition in Section 1, which states that all information disclosed and marked "Confidential" is Confidential Information. By adding the exception, the modified Section 3(b) creates ambiguity about what constitutes Confidential Information, directly conflicting with and undermining the broad definition provided in Section 1. This creates an in-text contradiction, as the Recipient has an obligation to protect the information under Section 1, but is now permitted to disclose it under certain circumstances in Section 3(b), thus following the definition of Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction, which says that Ambiguities occur when key terms or obligations are defined inconsistently in different parts of a contract, making enforcement difficult or interpretation unclear.

perturbation.original_text: 4. Ownership and Return of Confidential Information. All Confidential Information (including all copies thereof) shall remain the property of Discloser. All copies shall bear all proprietary markings contained on or in the originals. Upon the request of Discloser, Recipient shall, at Recipient’s option, either (a) return such materials to Discloser, or (b) certify the destruction thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. Ownership and Return of Confidential Information. All Confidential Information (including all copies thereof) shall remain the property of Discloser, unless the Recipient has significantly improved upon it. All copies shall bear all proprietary markings contained on or in the originals. Upon the request of Discloser, Recipient shall, at Discloser's option, either (a) return such materials to Discloser, or (b) certify the destruction thereof.
perturbation.explanation: The modification adds a condition to ownership: if the Recipient 'significantly improved' the Confidential Information, it seems to imply that the Recipient gains some form of ownership over the improvements. This contradicts the initial statement asserting that 'All Confidential Information... shall remain the property of Discloser,' creating ambiguity regarding ownership rights. This contradicts the first sentence.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4. Ownership and Return of Confidential Information. All Confidential Information (including all copies thereof) shall remain the property of Discloser, unless the Recipient has significantly improved upon it. All copies shall bear all proprietary markings contained on or in the originals. Upon the request of Discloser, Recipient shall, at Discloser's option, either (a) return such materials to Discloser, or (b) certify the destruction thereof.
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds 'unless the Recipient has significantly improved upon it' to the clause stating 'All Confidential Information... shall remain the property of Discloser'. This creates a direct contradiction with the contradicted text, which is the same as the changed text in this scenario. The added condition implies that the Recipient gains some form of ownership over the improvements, conflicting with the original statement and creating ambiguity regarding ownership rights. The added clause directly contradicts the beginning of the clause itself.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Rights. No rights or licenses to trademarks, inventions, copyrights, patents or any other intellectual property rights are implied or granted under this Agreement. Subject to the obligations of this Agreement, neither party will be precluded from independently developing technology or pursuing business opportunities similar to those covered by the Confidential Information disclosed hereunder. Each party retains sole discretion to assign or reassign the job responsibilities of its employees. Each party acknowledges that the other party may currently or in the future be developing information internally, or receiving information from third parties, that is similar to the Confidential Information. Nothing in this Agreement will prohibit Recipient from developing or having developed for it products, concepts, systems or techniques that are similar to those contemplated by or embodied in the Confidential Information, provided the Recipient does not violate its obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Rights. No rights or licenses to trademarks, inventions, copyrights, patents or any other intellectual property rights are implied or granted under this Agreement, except for the right to use the Discloser's Confidential Information for internal evaluation purposes. Subject to the obligations of this Agreement, neither party will be precluded from independently developing technology or pursuing business opportunities similar to those covered by the Confidential Information disclosed hereunder. Each party retains sole discretion to assign or reassign the job responsibilities of its employees. Each party acknowledges that the other party may currently or in the future be developing information internally, or receiving information from third parties, that is similar to the Confidential Information. Nothing in this Agreement will prohibit Recipient from developing or having developed for it products, concepts, systems or techniques that are similar to those contemplated by or embodied in the Confidential Information, provided the Recipient does not violate its obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The modification in Section 5 grants the Recipient the 'right to use the Discloser's Confidential Information for internal evaluation purposes.' While seemingly benign, this right directly contradicts the restrictions in Section 3, which dictate how the Recipient *shall* use the information (only to accomplish the Purpose, which is evaluating a potential business relationship). This new right broadens the scope of permitted use without defining its limits, conflicting with the purpose limitation.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3. Restrictions/Obligations. Recipient shall: (a) only disclose the Confidential Information to those employees and contractors with a need to know; provided that Recipient binds those employees and contractors to terms at least as restrictive as those stated in this Agreement (or, with respect to professional advisors, advises them of its obligations with respect to the Confidential Information); (b) not disclose any Confidential Information to any third party, without Discloser’s prior written consent, unless the third party is a potential investor; (c) use such Confidential Information only to the extent required to accomplish the Purpose; (d) not reproduce Confidential Information in any form except as required to accomplish the Purpose; (e) not reverse engineer, decompile, or disassemble any software disclosed by the Discloser; (f) not directly or indirectly export or transmit any Confidential Information to any country to which such export or transmission is restricted by regulation or statute; and (g) promptly provide the Discloser with notice of any actual or threatened breach of the terms of this Agreement. However, Recipient may disclose Confidential Information in accordance with a judicial or other governmental order provided that Recipient shall give Discloser written notice prior to such disclosure. Subject to Recipient’s obligations of non-disclosure, Recipient shall be free to use the residuals resulting from the use or access to the Confidential Information of Discloser. The term “residuals” means information in intangible form which is retained in the unaided memory of persons who have had access to the Confidential Information, without reference to such information in tangible or fixed form. Solely with respect to non-technical Confidential Information, Recipient’s obligations under this Section 3 shall expire one (1) year from the date of disclosure of such non-technical Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The original text in Section 5 states that no rights or licenses are granted under the agreement. The changed text introduces an exception, granting the right to use the Discloser's Confidential Information for internal evaluation purposes. This directly contradicts Section 3, which restricts the use of Confidential Information to only accomplish the 'Purpose' (evaluating a potential business relationship). The changed text broadens the scope of permissible use beyond this defined 'Purpose', creating an in-text contradiction as defined by the 'Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction' perturbation type.

perturbation.original_text: 6. Term and Termination. This Agreement shall remain in full force and effect for a period of three (3) years unless terminated sooner as set forth below. This Agreement may be terminated by either party upon thirty (30) days written notice, provided however, that Paragraphs 3, 4, 5, and 7 shall survive any termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. Term and Termination. This Agreement shall remain in full force and effect for a period of three (3) years unless terminated sooner as set forth below. This Agreement may be terminated by either party upon thirty (30) days written notice, provided however, that only Paragraphs 3, 4, and 7 shall survive any termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing Paragraph 5 from the list of surviving paragraphs after termination, the change introduces a contradiction. The original agreement ensures that rights concerning intellectual property developed during the agreement persist even after termination. The modification suggests that those rights terminate, creating ambiguity and a direct conflict with the intended ongoing protection, because paragraph 5 ensures the protection of intelectual property, and with this perturbation now the protection isn't longer ensured. This affects the obligations in paragraph 3 too.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5. Rights. No rights or licenses to trademarks, inventions, copyrights, patents or any other intellectual property rights are implied or granted under this Agreement, except for the right to use the Discloser's Confidential Information for internal evaluation purposes. Subject to the obligations of this Agreement, neither party will be precluded from independently developing technology or pursuing business opportunities similar to those covered by the Confidential Information disclosed hereunder. Each party retains sole discretion to assign or reassign the job responsibilities of its employees. Each party acknowledges that the other party may currently or in the future be developing information internally, or receiving information from third parties, that is similar to the Confidential Information. Nothing in this Agreement will prohibit Recipient from developing or having developed for it products, concepts, systems or techniques that are similar to those contemplated by or embodied in the Confidential Information, provided the Recipient does not violate its obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text ensures that paragraph 5 (Rights) survives the termination of the agreement, protecting intellectual property rights. The changed text removes paragraph 5 from the list of surviving paragraphs, implying that those rights do not survive termination. This directly contradicts the provisions in paragraph 5 that outline intellectual property rights and their continued protection, creating a strong in-text contradiction, and also affecting obligations in Paragraph 3.
